speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I want to continue where we stopped. And these fellows would get stone drunk, and ride on their horses through the town, and beat up anybody that wasn't fast enough to pull the merchandise back into their little stores. They usually slept behind the store and closed these heavy wooden doors that they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which town was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in a small town called Rozniatow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- R-O-Z with a certain accent N-I-A-T-O-W. It was near Lemberg. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And so that is also an illustration of the, the mindset and the mind of these people that made these Jewish storekeepers-- who, by no means, were affluent-- as the victim of their aggressive behavior that came through when they were drunk. And there was a cry that if-- let us say, if, for some reason or other, a Jew drove a car through the town and hit a Gentile child or something like that, the crime-- Polish word, [POLISH]. A Jew killed a Polish man, a Pollack. And then the same thing would happen, regardless of whether they were drunk or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you find a parallel between that what happened in Brooklyn, it's nothing new, OK? You know, when the rabbi ran into a child. This is traditional. This has been here before. And it will be here again. Because life continues to flow, in my view of it. As you watched this occurring, what, what, what went through your mind? And what did you say to your uncle? And what did he say to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Well, my uncle was in a different sphere. My uncle was the revered physician in town. Already, police officials brought their girlfriends to him for abortions. So he was very, very needed and very revered in this town. And he was a wonderful physician. And he was strictly in the company of the intelligentsia and the industrialists. So really, I guess we just chalked it up to what is called, [GERMAN], which is [POLISH], which is people. That's the German-- Austrian word for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay with your uncle after this pogrom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it occurred while I was there, during those almost three months that I was there. And this would happen invariably on those days where there was what is called, [GERMAN]. That means the big market.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which means that once a month, they would come from the outskirts to buy their clothing and their furnishings. And that would always be the final outcome-- invariably. People knew that. They would try to close their shutters. But if they got caught, then it was too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you took this, this experience back with you to Vienna. Did you tell your parents about what had witnessed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't go through it. I, I-- we didn't go through such details.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was traveling most of the time. And then came school. And then came this intensive study again. And I just absorbed it as part of what you learn in life as to how, to use the word, 'common people' behave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were, were politics discussed in your home between your parents and, and you when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty much. Oh, my father was sympathetic to the socialists. My mother was very traditional. And so they didn't agree on those things. But nothing on deeper level, just in general.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so we find ourselves now in 1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hitler has--

speaker: interviewee
text: Rolled in-- steamrolled in--

speaker: interviewer
text: --has rolled in without--

speaker: interviewee
text: --without any opposition whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened from that day forward in terms of how your non-Jewish neighbors perceived you? Did anything change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we, we-- with our neighbors things continued as before. There was a watchmaker in the building who had his shop up above. His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. His two sons were motorcycle buffs. I had a good relationship with them. Nothing changed on that level. But in school, we-- as I said before, we started to feel this problem. We had to re-register and get permission to continue our studies, which was denied to some for whatever reason. Maybe they had a political background that didn't, didn't find approval.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Crystal Night changed everything. Before we get to, to the-- to Kristallnacht--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the Crystal Night, I had no real happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was never called to wash sidewalks. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw them, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that generally done? Were people just taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would just pick people from the street that looked Jewish. They asked them if they were Jews and then rounded them up, and gave them a bucket and a-- and water, and pail, and said, wash this.

speaker: interviewer
text: People would just--

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: The non-Jews would just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me tell you. It's very simple. It's the old story, why didn't you resist? As this thing incrementally became worse and worse, the same thing happened in Dachau. I will explain it to you. The thought of resisting never occurred. It was assumed that it would be totally futile and met with instant violence.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jews basically are not violent people. That's been my experience. And so this idea-- what were we to resist? So the guy had us wash the sidewalk. Then when they started to smash the store, the windows. How are you going to resist?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a gun at home, which he needed for his travels. And when the Nazis came and there was this strong-- all the guns had to be surrendered. We figured, well, that's not the smartest thing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we wrapped it into a brown bag. And my brother and I walked across the very bridge that we lived nearby. And we tossed the bag back and forth like a ball, fooling around. And then one guy tossed harder. It went it over the railing. And there was the end of our gun. What were we to do, shoot one guy?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was no such thing as an organized effort, or even a thought to do something. The thought was to save your life. Go to the legation, stand in front in line, wait until your number is up. Go through the medical examination, which was most rigorous from top to bottom. And I mean bottom in the colloquial sense. They looked into your eyes when they had an infection. They looked everywheres because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I, I don't know what the edict was in those days to the legation-- it was how to screen people, or whom to admit, or what have you. I'm sure it wasn't give everybody a visa like the Chinese did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was really the, the, the driving force in life. We stood in line for hours just to get to the door, to hand in and see whether the application was correct. What other documents do you need, things like that. And then to satisfy the German authorities with all the documents.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the taxes had to be paid. We had certification. Even youngsters had to certify that they had paid all their taxes, and whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so in 1938, your father stood on line with the rest--

speaker: interviewee
text: We did it for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did it. We stood on line at the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you brother, Walter?

speaker: interviewee
text: --at the-- and my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: At which consulate?

speaker: interviewee
text: United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people stood in line with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were lines on the street snaking around the building, trying to get the applications in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you finally get your application in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably two days in all by the time we got everything correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed in line two days?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It took two days. We stayed in line. And they closed. And we got additional stamps, and what have you. And finally we got our application in. And then you're only examined when you get the visa, not beforehand. It's a big gamble.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you, along those lines, about the--

speaker: interviewee
text: But the, the major part of the time was taken up writing reasonably English letters to our relatives--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: --here.

speaker: interviewer
text: For sponsorship?

speaker: interviewee
text: For sponsorship, precisely. Because they had to certify that we would not become a public charge. And that was the reason why when we came here, I stayed in New York, and my brother and sister went to Chicago. We had a-- my father had a cousin in New York, a distant cousin, Klein. As a humorous aside, they were the second-largest manufacturer of Catholic prayer books and rosaries in the United States. And they were Jewish. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they, they made out an affidavit for my brother and myself. And our relative in Chicago-- Sabbath. Sabbath was a senator in the United States for-- he was the oldest in tenure until he was upseated by this fellow from Maine. And his brother-in-law had a department store in Chicago. And he issued an affidavit to myself and my brother. And both of them wrote that once we get on to our feet, we will be able to bring our sister in. This should give you the idea of their perception of the need for immediate assistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wrote to either one of them that we have now two affidavits for the boys and none for the girl. Would somebody please come across? And the fellow in New York refused. He said, the job market-- which was true-- was very bad. And the fellow in Chicago, my mother's relative, immediately sent in the affidavit for my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my parents, unfortunately, being Czech quota, were supposed to wait.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to ask you about the Evian Conference, the conference where all of the-- many, many of the countries got together to discuss the immigration problem. Were you aware in Vienna that this existed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not aware. No. I really am not familiar with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm being honest with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. And so-- and so you didn't know what, what efforts were going on in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: --outside--

speaker: interviewee
text: --what--

speaker: interviewer
text: --world.

speaker: interviewee
text: --what joint efforts were going on outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Needless to say, if it was after the Anschluss, it probably couldn't be printed anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain that. Because of censorship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the censorship of all newspapers. And we didn't have a shortwave radio in those days. So we had to swallow hook, line, and sinker whatever was fed-- spoon-fed to us over the airwaves or in the newspapers. It's a-- I will say that there were-- before the Anschluss, there were prominent Jewish journalists. There were newspapers in Vienna that were the equivalent of the both intellectually as well as in coverage. But all that quickly changed because the new order took over everything, just like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think about the outside world and their reaction to the Anschluss? What would have happened, for instance, if the European--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we knew about--

speaker: interviewer
text: --countries would, would object?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Brit-- the, the famous British fellow with the umbrella settling with Czechoslovakia saying, peace in our time. We saw that in the newspapers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you speaking of Chamberlain now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] little by little, it sunk in that with this chap, you can't negotiate. He wouldn't stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to move on to November of 1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I mentioned before briefly, there was a, a small temple, a shul. In one corner of the huge yard that was-- inside in Austria, almost every building in those days was around a big yard. So that the horse-drawn carriages could drive in. And still today, when you go there, cars are in there now. And at around 4:00 or 5:00 in the morning, we heard this tremendous commotion. And to me, it felt that the Nazis were demolishing the shul, which was par for the course. But shortly thereafter, there was a bang on our door. They'd obviously asked the superintendent where there were Jewish boys left. And she must have told them. Because they came straight to our door. They banged on the door. They came in with these broken chair legs. Each one of them 6 foot 6, black SS men. And they ripped open all the doors in the room. My father and mother were, of course, hysterical. And they ripped all of the drawers out, threw everything on the floor, looking for the proverbial money that Jews are hiding everywhere. Of course, we had jewelry. They didn't take that. And then they commandeered my brother and I, took us down. And my brother wore the bowler. I have a photo here of him with his bowlers. Because it was the cool dress in Vienna in those days for a young man. And I wore a sports cap. And as we walking in front of them-- obviously to scare us that we shouldn't gang up on them and resist or anything, they hit both of us over the head. Now, my brother's bowler sort of reduced the impact. But my cap permitted that chair leg to go right through and split my head open. I was bleeding profusely. We came in front of a door. And there was this-- it may sound funny, but there was this huge, convertible, black Mercedes standing there. Otherwise magnificent, if it weren't for the situation at the moment. And one was driving. And we were sitting in that second seat. And the second-- the other one was sitting in the backseat. And he-- we were driving down to the police station, the very street that I have walked and sauntered and skipped down to go to both of my schools. And here it was, 6:00 in the morning. And when they-- when we came to the police station, there was nobody there. A lone policeman was manning it. And they said to him, let's put these fellows in a cell. And I was bleeding profusely. And this cop-- try to use the term-- looked at me. And I said to him in Viennese slang, [NON-ENGLISH]. Which means like, hey buddy, come along, do me the favor. And he did. And he said to these people, you can't go in here. We have to first take their names and addresses. And believe it or not, these two big guys listened to him and left. I have the feeling that this man saved our lives. Because if he had opened the cell door and let us in there with these two fellows, there's no telling what they would have done to us. I really must stress this because it shows that there is humanity if you can only reach it. Well, little by little, the cell started to fill up-- old people, young people. We were just packed in there like sardines into this one room. And finally, toward the end of the day, they called us out. There was no bandage on my head, nothing, just a cap glued to the head with the coagulated blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a judge sitting on an elevated portion. And he called each one out and listened. What's your name, where you're from, what you are doing? To the right. I came. He said, what happened to you? I said, I fell down the stairs. The left-- everybody on the left went to Dachau. Everybody on the right went home. My brother went home.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No explanation for life. We were taken to a military-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Kaserne, military building. And we stayed there for two nights. I believe this was Friday night. And the Jews in the room, they're organizing a minyan. I wasn't religious. They delegated me to stay at the door and look through the peephole if anybody was coming. And they were praying. And after those two days, we were taken to the railroad station. And we were herded into regular railroad cars. I was sitting on the floor. We were ordered to look at the light continuously, obviously as a means of almost-- not hypnotizing, but getting us focused on one thing so that the guards wouldn't have any hard time with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were joking around. One of them hit me with the butt of a rifle over here and started to kid me. What were you doing? And I told him I was studying engineering. And they kidded me about many things.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they didn't abuse us in any other way. They were all youngsters. They couldn't have been more than 17, 18, in these new fancy uniforms, having the order to get us to some point where they transferred us onto the-- into the cattle cars. And it was there that I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I helped many of the older people get up onto this cattle car. Remember, from the street-- from the ground onto this door of the cattle car is quite a distance. And these old people couldn't negotiate. And they would hit them with the rifle butt. Speed up, speed up. I would stand-- be standing there. And I was the only one, I must admit, who grabbed them by the seat and helped them up to climb in there. And they didn't-- they somehow let me do it. I can't believe it. And as I look back, it was a stupid thing to do. But I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who stood guard over you? Were these Austrian--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the same--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this Austrian-- these were Austrian soldiers? These were German soldiers? You don't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you. But they were all in the black SS uniform.

words.text: But they were all in the black

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you the nationality. They could have been Czech, for what I know, or Pollacks. Who knows who volunteered once this great thing happened, don't you see? The mass hysteria.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were, were in the cattle cars? In each car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in-- on the car, it says, 6 horses or 48 soldiers. But I assure you, it was more. It was a trip that seemed to take forever. The stench was terrible. People were fainting. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But I was young and strong. I was a healthy young man. And we finally-- we didn't know where we were going. Rumors-- they're sending us to Siberia. They're sending us to Bremen to be shipped out. Who knows?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the doors finally opened, it was on a pleasant countryside. We were asked to get out. Younger people jumped out happily. Older people climbed out as best as they could. And we formed a marching column. And then marched into this magnificent, filigreed, iron gate. 'Arbeit macht frei,' 'work will make you free.' And we were assembled on this huge campground. And there, in the middle of this campground, was something like a chair. I-- I'm stressing this because this is important. And here they took this one fellow that they had standing on the side. And they announced-- and they must have had loudspeakers by then because a lot of the announcements were on the speaker. This gentleman was found with several false passports in his possession. He has to be punished. They announced it like this. They strapped him over this torture chair. And two guys took turns beating him mercilessly until he collapsed. As I think back, there was a purpose to this. It was to imbue in us the thought of terror. This is what can happen to you if you don't kowtow, if you don't behave, if you do anything wrong. And this was just a demonstration. It was a simple device, an object lesson-- object, they say, an object-- to make us realize this is not a summer camp. If you don't behave, forget it. And that's how it worked. We were put into different buildings. Dachau was new then. Everything was immaculate. Our washroom was immaculate. The building had wooden bunks with straw. I slept on a lower one. We used to have two, three guys in each bunk. It was about this wide. [POINTS] We were issued toothpaste and a toothbrush. I want you to understand getting to that German mentality. Our head was shaven, which was difficult in my case because was-- hair was sticking to my blood. But he did a reasonable job. And the next time he took it all off. And we were warned that there is an unpredictable inspection of toothbrushes. If the toothbrush is dirty, you will be punished. Now we saw what punishment meant. Believe me, it was amazing. And I was assigned to, to be a food carrier. Every time the bell rang, some of us would run to the kitchen, which I want to tell you was stainless steel from A to Z. It was organized to the nth degree. And we would get this soup or this chicory coffee in this huge tub. And two would run down this long runway to-- our building was almost in the back. And then we would ladle it out. We weren't permitted to eat. We would ladle it out to the guys standing at this huge table with a little tin dish. Sunday we had soup. But on the way back, we were permitted to eat whatever was left in these huge drums. We had our tin cup. It was the chicory coffee with a piece of dark bread. Or it was the soup on Sunday. It was the probably muggy-chicken flavored. [LAUGHS] There was no chicken in it. But it smelt like and tasted like chicken. We were permitted to take as much as we could on the way back. And that's how the food carriers didn't lose weight. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the rest of the day, we marched continuously. We marched from morning till night. We weren't-- we weren't-- I mean, being a food carrier, that was my assignment. But they weren't really digging ditches or anything like this in those days. In those days, it was a detention camp. I'm not a hero. I wasn't in a camp where they gassed or exterminated people. It was a detention camp. It was the beginning of what is referred to as the Final Solution. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our camp-- we were issued this pyjama with the blue stripes. And we had to keep it clean, immaculately clean. Our camp commander died in a car accident on Christmas night. It was bitter cold, snow on the ground. We had the stand at attention a night with spotlights on us, thousands of people on this huge assembly ground, with SS people marching down the rows and looking in to see if anybody would move. I moved my fingers and my toes so not to freeze. Anybody that was caught with paper in his blue pyjamas was severely punished. People would find papers and stuff them in as insulation against the cold. It took tremendous self-control to stand up under those circumstances. That was the worst night of them all, I will say. The camp population varied from homosexuals to socialists to Jews, which did not exclude the other groups. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The people that they made us march and do exercises were Jews. They were selected by the camp people, either due to their military background or their looks and demeanors to be our supervisors. And they were very straight and strict. If you marched, if you didn't stay in the bathroom because you wanted to avoid the cold-- and a lot of young people did and froze their feet off. They couldn't get their feet into the shoes anymore that we were issued because they had swollen up from the cold. It was the wrong thing-- the worst thing to do to try to escape the daily routine. The marching actually kept you healthy. I'll be honest with you. And the reduced calories that you got, it didn't seem to do you any harm. Think of it that way. My bunk-- my commander was a German socialist, a huge fellow, a young fellow, a magnificent chap. He tried to protect us. He warned us of inspections. He tried to protect us in the best way. It's interesting how the good in some humans comes through if it's there, no matter what. And if the bad is there, it cannot be suppressed. And the days went by like this. I know one day-- we were permitted to get money from home, believe it or not. There was a-- we were-- we were not permitted to get any goods from home, but money, yes. And there was a general store. And I bought myself sardines every day as a supplement to my food. I don't know where my parents got the money for me because things were getting very rough. But my mother managed to send me that little bit of money every two weeks. And I bought myself a can of sardines whenever I could. And it stuck with me. To this day, I eat sardines. Although now they are without salt or any water. [LAUGHS] But somehow, I [LAUGHS] caught on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents know that you were sent to Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were called into a huge room, we were sat down, and dictated what to send home. My dear blank, blank. I am well. I feel fine-- platitudes like this. And that's-- went to my parents. And on top it said, [GERMAN], which means, protective custody Jew. In those days, we didn't have to be called Sarah and whatever, Moshe, whatever. Every Jew had to take on this name. Carl Hirsch and my address-- and it was there that they were able to send me letters, which were censored. They opened every letter. And they prescribed on the postcard that I was able to send them how many lines, how many sentences, in what form the letter had to be kept.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll continue on.
